title: Halpert's Booking
tagline: A hotel booking interface built with nothing but HTML5 and CSS3 — a proof that good layout doesn't need a framework.
aboutHeadline: A static site that takes layout seriously.
blurb: Halpert's Booking is a fully static hotel-booking interface written with zero JavaScript and zero front-end frameworks — just HTML5, CSS3, Flexbox, Grid, and a strict BEM naming discipline. The goal was to see how far a modern hotel-booking experience could be pushed without reaching for React or a utility library: a gradient navbar with hover affordances, a destination grid, a sticky helper rail on search results, and a fully styled calendar — all rendered server-free and pixel-stable.
pullQuotes: Most of what passes for 'we needed a framework' is really 'we didn't trust our HTML'.
The browser already knows how to do most of this. The trick is staying out of its way.
Layout is a craft, not a library.

highlights.title: Gradient nav & hero
highlights.lede: The landing page that sets the brand.
highlights.body: A full-bleed hero with a gradient navbar, a search-prompt strip, and a destination carousel — all rendered with Flexbox and CSS transitions. Hover states are pure :hover; no JS, no flicker.

shots.caption: Gradient navbar, full-bleed hero, hover affordances.
Sticky helper rail and result cards.

highlights.title: Destination grid
highlights.body: Destinations are surfaced as a responsive Grid of cards, each with its own micro-interaction on hover. The layout reflows cleanly from a 4-column desktop to a 1-column mobile without a single media query for individual cards — just container-driven sizing.

shots.caption: Search results follow the same layout system.

highlights.title: Results & sticky helper
highlights.lede: A results page that respects the eye.
highlights.body: The search-results layout pairs a scrollable card list with a sticky helper rail that stays anchored as the user scrolls — implemented entirely with `position: sticky` and a vertical Flexbox column. No JS, no scroll listeners, no layout shift.

shots.caption: Calendar slides in for date picking.

highlights.title: Styled CSS date picker
highlights.lede: A calendar without a calendar library.
highlights.body: A fully styled date-picker built from a CSS Grid and `<input type='date'>`'s native affordances — restyled aggressively with `accent-color` and `::-webkit-calendar-picker-indicator` to match the rest of the brand. No flatpickr, no Pikaday, no React Date Range.

challengesHeadline: What you learn when you can't reach for JavaScript.

challenges.title: Building a responsive layout without utility classes
challenges.body: No Tailwind, no Bootstrap, no inline styles. Every breakpoint was hand-tuned through media queries on container widths rather than viewport widths — which forced a much cleaner mental model of which components needed to reflow and which could just shrink. The result was fewer breakpoints than a typical Tailwind build.

challenges.title: Maintaining visual consistency without templating
challenges.body: Four hand-written HTML pages, no Pug, no JSX, no shared header component. The discipline was BEM-naming every reusable block and keeping a single `styles.css` so any visual tweak landed everywhere in one shot. The lesson: consistency is a CSS architecture problem, not a templating problem.
